recommendation.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable part of the canopy or fruiting structures, with increased risk of fruit set loss and postharvest quality reduction.
treatment: Prune and remove infected plant material more thoroughly to reduce ongoing inoculum.
Use integrated management combining sanitation, canopy management, and preventative protection of healthy tissue where locally approved.
During flowering and fruit set, pay particular attention to disease suppression because infection at these stages can strongly affect fruit development.
For harvested fruit, validated postharvest management options may be used where available to reduce lesion development during storage and handling.
prevention: Reduce humidity within the canopy through pruning and spacing where feasible.
Remove fallen infected debris and other sources of persistent inoculum.
Plan management around weather conditions, especially wet periods that increase infection risk.
monitoring: Inspect every 3 to 5 days during favorable disease periods.
Track whether lesions are increasing on flowers, panicles, or fruit.
caution: Anthracnose can remain latent and become more obvious later, including after harvest.
Relying only on late-stage intervention is less effective than integrated early management.

Management of Post-Harvest Anthracnose: Current Approaches and Future Perspectives

Management of Post-Harvest Anthracnose: Current Approaches and Future Perspectives

recommendation.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Maintain good canopy airflow through appropriate pruning.
Monitor the tree during humid and rainy periods when fungal diseases are more likely to develop.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness where possible.
monitoring: Inspect the tree again within 7 to 14 days.
Reassess if spots, powdery growth, or fruit lesions appear.
caution: A healthy-looking leaf can still develop symptoms later, especially under favorable disease conditions.

references.title: General orchard sanitation and disease prevention practices for mango production

recommendation.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable portion of the foliage, with increasing lower-canopy infection and clear risk of additional spread under humid conditions.
treatment: Remove heavily infected leaves and destroy infected plant material away from the growing area.
Use integrated management combining sanitation, humidity reduction, and locally approved protective treatment where appropriate.
Where available, supportive biological control strategies may be included as part of a broader disease management plan.
prevention: Increase spacing and ventilation where possible to reduce moisture retention on leaves.
Avoid irrigation practices that increase humidity or extended foliage wetness.
Use resistant cultivars where suitable options are available, while recognizing that resistance can be overcome by changing pathogen populations.
monitoring: Inspect every 3 to 5 days during humid or enclosed growing conditions.
Track whether infection is moving upward through the canopy or increasing on new leaves.
caution: Leaf mold can become difficult to suppress once humidity remains consistently high and sporulation is widespread.
Resistance-based management should be supported by continued monitoring because pathogen race variation is known to occur.

recommendation.summary: Early late blight symptoms are visible on a limited portion of foliage, typically as water-soaked dark lesions, but disease spread is still relatively localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected foliage where disease is still limited.
Apply locally approved preventative protection effective against oomycete pathogens to protect healthy foliage.
Strengthen crop protection immediately if cool, wet, or foggy weather is expected.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and reduce prolonged leaf wetness.
Water at the base of the plant where possible and avoid practices that increase splash dispersal.
Monitor nearby potato and tomato plants because linked spread can occur under favorable conditions.
monitoring: Inspect again within 24 to 48 hours during favorable weather.
Check whether lesions are expanding or whether pale or white sporulation appears around lesion margins.
caution: Late blight spreads rapidly under cool and wet conditions, so early intervention is critical.
Because Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete rather than a true fungus, management should rely on measures validated for oomycete control.

recommendation.summary: TYLCV is widespread, with extensive leaf curling, yellowing, stunting, and high risk of major yield loss across the crop.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants to reduce virus reservoirs for continued whitefly transmission where practical.
Focus management on protecting remaining healthy plants through strict whitefly suppression and sanitation.
Do not rely on curative treatment of infected plants; prioritize prevention of further spread.
prevention: For future planting, prioritize resistant cultivars carrying validated TYLCV resistance genes where available.
Use only clean transplants and reduce whitefly exposure at the nursery and field level.
Plan future crop cycles around integrated vector management, reflective mulch where feasible, and sanitation.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding tomato plants and nearby susceptible hosts frequently during active outbreaks.
Continue monitoring whitefly abundance even after removing symptomatic plants.
caution: Severe TYLCV outbreaks can cause major yield loss and are difficult to reverse once widely established.
Long-term management depends more on resistance and vector control than on treatment after symptom expression.

recommendation.summary: Late blight is widespread, with extensive foliage infection and high risk of complete canopy loss, tuber infection, and severe yield reduction.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material promptly to reduce inoculum pressure and protect surrounding plants.
Do not compost heavily infected plant material unless safe disposal guidance specifically permits it.
Focus management on limiting further spread and protecting nearby healthy plants rather than attempting to recover severely damaged tissue.
prevention: For future planting, prioritize resistant or less susceptible cultivars where suitable options are available.
Remove volunteer potatoes and maintain strict sanitation after the season.
Plan future management around early preventative action and forecasting support rather than delayed response.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor harvested tubers carefully for signs of late blight infection during storage.
caution: Severe late blight can lead to major field loss and postharvest tuber problems.
Under high disease pressure, integrated control and resistance are more reliable than relying on a single intervention.

recommendation.summary: Early late blight symptoms are visible on a limited portion of foliage, typically as water-soaked dark lesions, but disease spread is still localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected leaves or shoots where disease is still limited.
Apply locally approved preventative protection effective against oomycete pathogens to protect healthy foliage.
Strengthen crop protection immediately when cool, wet, or foggy weather is expected.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and reduce prolonged leaf wetness.
Water at the base of the plant where possible.
Inspect nearby tomato and potato plants because linked spread can occur under favorable conditions.
monitoring: Inspect again within 24 to 48 hours during favorable disease conditions.
Check lesion margins and the underside of leaves for pale or white sporulating growth.
caution: Late blight spreads rapidly under cool and wet conditions, so early intervention is critical.
Because Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete rather than a true fungus, management should be based on measures validated for oomycete control.

recommendation.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable portion of the foliage, with clear risk of rapid spread to additional leaves, stems, and nearby susceptible plants.
treatment: Remove heavily infected foliage and destroy infected plant debris away from the growing area.
Use integrated management combining preventative oomycete-directed protection, sanitation, and strict moisture control.
Where available, use disease forecasting or decision-support guidance to improve timing of intervention.
prevention: Eliminate volunteer tomato or potato plants and other possible sources of carryover inoculum where relevant.
Reduce leaf wetness duration and improve air movement around plants where possible.
Plan management around preventive action rather than waiting for severe spread.
monitoring: Inspect every 1 to 2 days during cool, wet, or highly favorable conditions.
Track lesion expansion and appearance of new water-soaked symptoms on upper foliage and stems.
caution: Preventative management is generally more effective than attempting recovery after rapid spread has begun.
Once disease becomes active across multiple leaves, delayed action can lead to major crop loss.

recommendation.summary: Late blight is widespread, with extensive foliage infection and high risk of major plant loss, fruit infection, and severe yield reduction.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material promptly to reduce inoculum pressure and protect surrounding plants.
Do not compost heavily infected plant material unless safe disposal guidance specifically permits it.
Focus management on limiting further spread and protecting nearby healthy plants rather than attempting to recover severely damaged tissue.
prevention: For future planting, prioritize resistant or less susceptible cultivars where suitable options are available.
Maintain strict sanitation and remove residual infected material after the season.
Use early preventative management and forecasting support where available in future crop cycles.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor fruit and any harvested material carefully for signs of continuing infection.
caution: Severe late blight can lead to major crop loss in a very short period under favorable weather.
Under high disease pressure, integrated control and host resistance are more reliable than relying on a single intervention.

recommendation.summary: Early powdery mildew symptoms are visible on a limited portion of leaves, flowers, or young tissues, and infection is still localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected tissues where infection is still limited.
Improve canopy airflow through light pruning and by avoiding excessive canopy density.
Where locally approved and appropriate, apply sulfur-based protective treatment as part of an integrated management approach.
Begin management early, especially during flowering and early fruit development, because these stages are highly vulnerable.
prevention: Monitor closely during conditions favorable for powdery mildew development, especially around flowering and young fruit stages.
Reduce prolonged humidity within the canopy where possible.
Avoid practices that keep young tissues damp for long periods.
monitoring: Inspect the tree again within 5 to 7 days during favorable weather.
Check whether white powdery growth is spreading to panicles, flowers, or young fruit.
caution: Powdery mildew can significantly reduce fruit set if not managed early.
Sulfur-based products should only be used according to locally approved guidance and label instructions.

recommendation.summary: Early anthracnose symptoms are visible on a limited portion of leaves, flowers, or young fruit, and disease spread is still relatively localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected twigs, leaves, flowers, or fruit to reduce inoculum pressure.
Improve canopy airflow through light pruning and avoid conditions that prolong leaf and flower wetness.
Where locally approved and appropriate, apply preventative fungicidal protection to healthy tissues as part of an integrated management program.
For postharvest fruit protection, validated biological or physical postharvest treatments may be considered where available.
prevention: Monitor closely during humid, rainy, or prolonged wet periods because these conditions favor infection and disease development.
Reduce canopy density where practical to lower humidity and improve drying.
Avoid practices that increase splash dispersal or prolonged surface moisture.
monitoring: Inspect again within 5 to 7 days during humid conditions.
Check whether symptoms are spreading to flowers, panicles, or developing fruit.
caution: Anthracnose management is more effective when started early rather than after extensive spread.
No single cultivar is fully resistant across all known anthracnose-causing strains.

recommendation.summary: Powdery mildew is widespread, with extensive infection affecting flowers, young fruit, and/or new vegetative growth, and there is high risk of severe fruit set reduction.
treatment: Remove heavily infected plant material where practical to reduce ongoing inoculum.
Use a full integrated management approach combining sanitation, canopy management, and validated protective treatment where locally approved.
Focus on protecting remaining healthy flowers, young fruit, and new growth rather than attempting to recover severely damaged tissues.
prevention: Maintain strict sanitation around infected trees and remove infected debris from the production area where feasible.
Strengthen monitoring during flowering and early fruit development, which are critical disease-sensitive stages.
Consider varietal susceptibility when selecting planting material for future seasons.
monitoring: Inspect frequently during favorable disease conditions and monitor nearby susceptible mango trees.
Assess the extent of fruit set loss and continued spread to new tissues.
caution: Severe powdery mildew can cause major loss of flowering success and fruit set.
No single management measure should be relied upon alone under severe disease pressure.

treatment: Remove heavily infected plant material where practical to reduce ongoing inoculum.

recommendation.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable part of the canopy or reproductive structures, with increasing risk to flowering, fruit set, and yield.
treatment: Prune and remove infected plant material more thoroughly to reduce inoculum pressure.
Use an integrated management strategy combining sanitation, canopy opening, and protective sulfur-based management where locally approved.
Pay particular attention to disease suppression during flowering and fruit set, since these stages are strongly affected by powdery mildew.
prevention: Reduce canopy humidity and improve air movement through pruning and spacing where possible.
Monitor susceptible growth flushes, flowers, and young fruit regularly.
For future planting decisions, prefer cultivars reported to be relatively less susceptible where locally suitable.
monitoring: Inspect every 3 to 5 days during active disease-conducive periods.
Track whether infection is spreading to additional panicles, flowers, or fruit.
caution: Delaying control until infection is widespread reduces effectiveness.
High doses or poorly timed sulfur-based treatments may increase the risk of phytotoxic effects.

references.title: Alleviating biotic stress of powdery mildew in mango cv. Keitt by sulfur nanoparticles and assessing their effect on productivity and disease severity
Powdery mildew of mango in South Africa: A review
Evaluation of Various Mango Varieties against the Infection Dynamics of Powdery Mildew

recommendation.summary: Early symptoms are visible on a limited number of leaves, usually as brown lesions with concentric ring patterns, often beginning on older or lower foliage.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected lower leaves where infection is still limited.
Apply locally approved protectant fungicidal treatment to healthy foliage as part of an integrated management program.
Where available, consider biological support options such as Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas fluorescens, or Trichoderma-based products supported by current evidence.
prevention: Reduce soil splash by mulching or maintaining a barrier between soil and foliage.
Prune lower leaves where appropriate to reduce contact with splash-dispersed inoculum.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness and improve airflow around the canopy.
monitoring: Inspect the plant again within 5 to 7 days under favorable disease conditions.
Check whether new lesions are appearing on additional lower leaves or petioles.
caution: Early blight management is generally more effective when started before symptoms become widespread.
Biological and plant-based approaches may support suppression, but should not be treated as guaranteed replacements for an integrated management program.

references.title: Biology, ecology, and epidemiology of Alternaria species associated with the tomato pathosystem: a review

recommendation.summary: Virus symptoms are clearly established on a noticeable part of the canopy, with increasing risk of spread through whitefly activity and significant reduction in plant vigor.
treatment: Remove symptomatic plants or severely affected plant parts where practical to reduce the local virus source.
Strengthen integrated whitefly management immediately using locally approved methods.
Maintain or introduce reflective mulch or comparable whitefly-deterrent measures where possible.
prevention: Continue using resistant cultivars and clean transplants as the main preventive strategy.
Control weeds and alternate hosts that may support whiteflies or virus reservoirs near the crop.
Avoid overlapping tomato plantings that sustain continuous vector and virus pressure.
monitoring: Inspect every 2 to 3 days during periods of active whitefly pressure.
Track spread to nearby plants and worsening of leaf curl, yellowing, and stunting.
caution: Once TYLCV is established, management is primarily about limiting spread and protecting remaining healthy plants.
Single-strategy management is less reliable than integrated resistance and vector control.

recommendation.summary: Late blight is widespread, with extensive foliage infection and high risk of complete canopy loss, tuber infection, and severe yield reduction.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material promptly to reduce inoculum pressure and protect surrounding plants.
Do not compost heavily infected plant material unless safe disposal guidance specifically permits it.
Focus management on limiting further spread and protecting nearby healthy plants rather than attempting to recover severely damaged tissue.
prevention: For future planting, prioritize resistant or less susceptible cultivars where suitable options are available.
Remove volunteer potatoes and maintain strict sanitation after the season.
Plan future management around early preventative action and forecasting support rather than delayed response.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor harvested tubers carefully for signs of late blight infection during storage.
caution: Severe late blight can lead to major field loss and postharvest tuber problems.
Under high disease pressure, integrated control and resistance are more reliable than relying on a single intervention.

recommendation.summary: Early bacterial spot symptoms are visible on a limited number of leaves or fruit, and infection is still localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected plant parts where infection is still limited.
Apply locally approved protectant treatments to healthy foliage as part of an integrated management program.
Where available, consider biological management options supported by evidence, such as selected Bacillus-based products or bacteriophage-based suppression strategies.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and reduce splash dispersal between plants.
Do not handle or prune plants when foliage is wet.
Use clean tools and sanitize them after contact with infected plants.
Start with clean seed or transplants and use validated seed sanitation methods where appropriate.
monitoring: Inspect plants again within 3 to 5 days during warm, humid, or rainy conditions.
Check whether lesions are appearing on new leaves, stems, or fruit.
caution: Copper-based products act mainly as protectants and may be less reliable where copper-tolerant bacterial populations are present.
Early intervention is more effective than waiting until infection becomes widespread.

recommendation.summary: Bacterial spot is widespread, with extensive lesions on foliage and/or fruit and high risk of major plant damage and further spread.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material to reduce inoculum pressure and protect nearby plants.
Use a full integrated management strategy focused on sanitation, splash reduction, clean planting material, and locally approved protective measures.
Plan future control around host resistance, clean seed/transplants, and stronger preventative management rather than relying only on in-season treatment.
prevention: Remove all infected crop debris at the end of the season.
Use disease-free seed or validated seed sanitation procedures before future planting.
Prefer resistant or less susceptible cultivars where locally suitable options exist.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor new flushes of growth and fruit for continued lesion development.
caution: Severe outbreaks can be difficult to reverse once disease is established across the crop.
Because copper tolerance and pathogen diversity are recognized problems, long-term management should emphasize integrated strategies and resistance where possible.

recommendation.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable part of the plant, with ongoing spread to new leaves or fruit and increased risk of yield and quality loss.
treatment: Remove heavily infected leaves and fruit where practical and destroy infected debris away from the crop area.
Use an integrated disease management approach combining sanitation, protectant treatment, and resistance-inducing or biological tools where available.
Where locally available and validated, induced-resistance strategies or resistance-inducing products may help reduce disease severity.
prevention: Reduce leaf wetness duration and minimize splash from irrigation or rain.
Improve spacing and airflow where possible.
Avoid repeated use of a single control strategy when resistance risk is known.
monitoring: Inspect every 2 to 3 days during disease-conducive weather.
Monitor nearby pepper or tomato plants for linked spread.
caution: Bacterial spot management is strongest when multiple control measures are combined rather than relying on one product alone.
Warm, humid conditions can accelerate spread.

recommendation.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Reduce soil splash by maintaining mulch or appropriate crop spacing where practical.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness and unnecessary overhead irrigation.
Monitor frequently during cool, wet, or humid periods that favor blight development.
monitoring: Inspect the crop again within 5 to 7 days.
Reassess if target-like lesions, water-soaked spots, or rapid canopy changes appear.
caution: Potato foliage can appear healthy before rapid disease onset under favorable weather conditions.

recommendation.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Maintain good airflow and avoid excessive canopy density.
Reduce soil splash with mulch or careful watering practices.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness and inspect regularly during humid conditions.
monitoring: Inspect the plant again within 5 to 7 days.
Reassess if spots, mold, curling, yellowing, or water-soaked lesions appear.
caution: Healthy leaves can still develop disease later, especially under favorable environmental conditions or vector pressure.
